response: The internet traces its origins to 1960s U.S. Department of Defense-funded computer networking research, which spawned ARPANET—its precursor—first demonstrated in 1969 with a message between UCLA and Stanford Research Institute; the network grew slowly through the 1970s, linking universities and research institutions. In 1983, adoption of TCP/IP protocols laid the modern internet’s technical foundation, while Tim Berners-Lee’s 1989 invention of the World Wide Web (with HTML, URLs, and HTTP) at CERN created its core user-facing framework. Mainstream accessibility arrived with 1993’s Mosaic browser, fueling the late-1990s dot-com boom (followed by a 2001 crash) that yielded high-value long-term survivors Amazon and Google; subsequent pivotal shifts included mid-2000s social media platforms (Facebook, Twitter, YouTube), 2006’s launch of AWS cloud computing, and 2007’s iPhone, which catalyzed ubiquitous mobile internet access. Today, the internet connects over 5 billion people globally, underpinning nearly every aspect of modern life from commerce and communication to entertainment.
